subject: Important: Ledger Security Verification Required
body:
Ledger identified a recent data incident. Please verify if your recovery phrase has been affected.
Ledger Security Notice – Verification Required
Ledger Support Team · October 14, 2025
Dear Ledger User,
On October 14, 2025, our security team identified unauthorized access to one of our non-transactional databases. Following an internal audit, we confirmed that some users’ recovery phrases (seed phrases) may have been exposed.
While your Ledger device remains secure, we ask all affected users to confirm their recovery status through our dedicated verification portal. This ensures your account integrity and allows immediate regeneration if exposure is detected.
Please take the following steps:
• Click the button below to open the secure verification portal.
• Follow the guided process to check if your recovery phrase was impacted.
• If compromised, you will be redirected to the regeneration and transfer page for immediate protection.
Verify My Recovery Phrase
The verification process is encrypted and performed through Ledger’s secure infrastructure. Please note: Ledger will never ask for your recovery phrase by email. If you receive such a request, ignore it and contact our support team immediately.
We deeply regret any concern this may have caused and appreciate your prompt action to secure your account.

Kind regards,
Ledger Support Team
Ledger, 27 Rue des Rosiers, 75004 Paris, France
© 2025 Ledger. All rights reserved.

subject: Urgent Improvement of Data Encryption and Access Controls
body: Respected Customer Support, I am contacting you to highlight my concerns regarding the current data encryption and access controls in place for medical records and the hospital's IT systems. Given the critical nature of patient data security, which can be compromised with severe repercussions, I strongly advocate for the swift enhancement of these security measures. This includes the adoption of strong encryption methods, the use of multi-factor authentication, and the implementation of stringent access controls. Moreover, it is imperative to perform regular security evaluations and penetration tests to detect and rectify any potential weaknesses. Could you please furnish me with details on the initiatives currently underway to bolster data encryption and access controls? I am eager to receive your feedback on the steps taken. I am looking forward to your response. Thank you for your swift action towards securing patient information.

subject: A home-stretch election vibe check
body: This Week in Politics Experts on Olivia Chow’s lead, and what else we should be talking about ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ THIS WEEK IN POLITICS By David Rider Richard Lautens/Toronto Star With advance voting underway and multiple debates in the rear view mirror, election day on June 26 is coming fast. To do a home-stretch vibe check, I put questions to three independent observers — here’s what they had to say. Do you believe polls showing Olivia Chow with a big lead? It’s significant that repeated polls over time suggest Chow is the frontrunner, Myer Siemiatycki, a Toronto Metropolitan University politics professor emeritus, told me. Others being clustered behind suggests “it has been difficult for the public to parse the differences or distinctiveness of their campaigns.” No rival has yet generated momentum to be an obvious alternative to Chow. Velma Morgan, chair of Operation Black Vote Canada, agreed. “It’s difficult to argue with the polls since they consistently favour (Chow), but we don’t know where the undecided will fall. We are also unaware of data on second choice for declared Chow supporters,” she said. She noted some political polls have turned out to be wrong. There’s no reason to doubt the polls, University of Western Ontario political scientist Zack Taylor said, but “we still have two weeks left, and people may start to tune in more, so movement is possible.” Don't miss a thing from your favourite Star columnists. Always get the latest from the voices that matter most to you: pick the columnists you'd like to follow here, and you’ll get an email every time they have a new article online. How could a non-Chow candidate break through? We’ll hear more “dire warnings” of a Chow mayoralty by rivals declaring themselves the only viable alternative, Siemiatycki predicted. “Doubling down on negativity” might be the only hope for those candidates, he said, but it hasn’t worked so far. Rivals need to draw in other candidates’ support and become the one voters coalesce around, Morgan said. The Chow campaign will try to woo supporters of other progressive candidates to ward off a centre-right candidate coming up the middle. Taylor is surprised no low-polling candidates have, so far, suspended their campaigns because of trouble fundraising, given the overlapping pools of donors. If that happens, it could help consolidate votes for those seen as the most likely to beat Chow. Are you surprised at which issues are getting traction? What deserves more attention? Morgan isn’t surprised safety, housing and transit dominate conversations, but wants to know how closely voters are examining proffered solutions. Mainstream media needs to help them understand candidate pledges, she said, and their potential impacts on disparate communities. Taylor noted issues include housing affordability, community reinvestment and public safety but says none have crystallized as the overriding public concern, although that may happen yet. The campaign has shown that, after years of John Tory’s rule, Torontonians are “open to new directions,” Siemiatycki said. In early campaign days, Ontario Place and the Science Centre dominated headlines, reflecting public unease with provincial plans. Housing, transportation and safety are also hot topics, Siemiatycki said, but he’d like to see candidates talk about countering provincial interventions: “How will they defend Toronto democracy?” Here’s more of the Star’s Toronto election coverage from my colleagues: How wide is Olivia Chow’s lead? Here’s what the latest polls show. Ben Spurr lists six takeaways from this week’s CBC-hosted debate, and I covered this week’s second debate on issues affecting older adults. Advance voting is now open — here’s where and when you can cast your ballot. Listen: Edward Keenan gets an insider look at the race from Jennifer Keesmaat. Olivia Chow’s property tax plan might add up, but she needs to show us the math, Matt Elliott writes. Finally, last week’s edition of This Week in Politics misspelled Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland’s name. David Rider is city hall bureau chief for the Star. Follow him on Twitter: @dmrider. subject: Problem with Concurrent Device Failures
body: Greetings customer service, I am contacting you to address a concern about multiple devices that have been experiencing simultaneous failures. The situation emerged abruptly, and I am considering the possibility of software conflicts or a malware infection. Despite attempting to reset the devices, updating the software, and performing a malware scan, the issue remains unresolved. I would greatly appreciate any guidance you can offer to help resolve this matter. Could you please suggest any additional troubleshooting steps I can take? Thank you for your attention and support.
